text: We are presenting a robot liver segment seven resection.
The case is a seven-year-old woman with past history of iodine allergy, choleritis and eradicated survivors infection and Charleston IV.
The patient had a forty-two millimeters hyperdense liver mass deep in segment seven.
The surgeon places a trocar in a straight line below the umbilicus, oriented towards the upper right quadrant of the abdomen.
The patient was placed in French position with left-sided decapitas.

The surgeon uses a 30-degree robotic optic to visualize the surgical field, employing a left bipolar grasper, a right monopolar scissor, and a double right tip-up grasper for precise manipulation and dissection.
The surgeon begins by clearing the winged loviatus to create a passage for the Pringles maneuver, preparing for liver-healer control.
The surgeon begins mobilizing the right liver lobe by dissecting the round ligament and falciform ligament, while clearing the root of the main hepatic veins.
The surgeon uses a robotic sealer to clear the roots of the hepatic veins, focusing on precise manipulation within the surgical field.
The surgeon sections the right triangular ligament, facilitating the complete mobilization of the right liver lobe.
The surgeon uses monopolar scissors to carefully remove tiny adhesions while preserving the diaphragm, ensuring there is no risk of pneumoperitoneum.
The surgeon carefully removes tumor adhesions from the liver, ensuring complete clearance of these structures at the current level of dissection.
The surgeon uses an aspirator tool as a retractor to evacuate smoke and blood, aiding in the mobilization of the right liver lobe.
The surgeon uses monopolar scissors to mark the specimen limits on the liver before proceeding with the resection.
The surgeon uses an endocyanin green scope to visualize the lesion beneath the liver surface, preparing for the subsequent intraoperative ultrasound examination.
The ultrasound image displays an undefined mass located deep in segment VII of the liver, characterized by indistinct margins and measuring approximately four centimeters in size.
The surgeon initiates dissection by making a perimetral incision along the marked margin limits using monopolar scissors.
The surgeon uses a clamp to gradually penetrate the parenchyma in a controlled manner, likely employing a crash-clamp technique.

The surgeon uses bipolar forceps and an aspirator tool in counter-attraction with monopolar scissors to reveal small vessels inside the bronchioma, which are individually coagulated with monopolar energy.
The surgeon maintains a convex transection plane to control small vessels, ensuring a clear surgical field during the procedure.
The surgeon progresses deeper into the bronchioma, where larger third-order hepatic vein branches become visible. These vessels may require hemlock clamping before being dissected.
The surgeon uses Maryland forceps to perform a sharp dissection, controlling and clearing vessels within the bronchioma before placing hemlock clamps.
The surgeon exposes the third order portal branch for segment VII, known as PE-VII, emerging from the deeper parenchyma within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses Maryland forceps to carefully dissect and control the PE-VII branch, placing hemlock clamps on the vessel before proceeding with its removal.
The surgeon maintains the right hepatic vein margin tightly during the medial limit of the resection, ensuring a precise anatomical resection.
The surgeon follows the dissection plane to the top, revealing the main segment seven branch of the hepatic vein, which is cleaned in preparation for robotic wrist stapler application.
The articulated jaws of the robotic instrument position the stapler suture precisely at the desired level within the surgical field.
The surgeon removes the specimen by transecting the remaining bronchial attachments, separating the segment from the rest of the liver after the hepatic vein has been taken.
The surgeon releases the hemostatic clamps and reviews hemostasis using bipolar forceps, occasionally placing hemostatic stitches to ensure proper blood flow control.
The surgeon uses the sixth degree of the under-wrist forceps to perform delicate hemostatic sutures within the bronchyma, ensuring precise control and stabilization of the tissue.
The surgeon uses bipolar forceps to control small bleeding, demonstrating the instrument's effectiveness in achieving hemostasis.
The surgeon optionally applies hemostatic patches to the liver surface to control bleeding before completing the liver resection.
Before this kind of hemostatic quality, we do not place drains after surgery so the patient may follow enhanced recovery after surgery in fast track guidelines.
The outcome was uneventful clavin one and postoperative day six and the exam revealed a intrahepatic cholangiocalcinoma of forty two millimeters with free oncological margins.
The patient followed adjuvant chemotherapy scheme with capacitopine.
Thank you very much.
